summary: In this landmark 1996 New England Journal of Medicine article, Julie E. Buring and her co-authors report the results of the Physicians' Health Study regarding beta carotene. They conclude that 12 years of beta carotene supplementation produced neither benefit nor harm in preventing cancer or cardiovascular disease in healthy men, emphasizing the necessity of long-term randomized trials over observational data for public health recommendations.

principles.statement: Observational studies are insufficient to prove the efficacy of dietary supplements; large, long-term randomized trials are required.
principles.rationale: Observational studies cannot easily distinguish whether apparent health benefits are due to a specific nutrient, other nutrients found in the same foods, or broader lifestyle characteristics of the people who consume them.
principles.source_quote: It is difficult to determine from observational studies, however, whether the apparent benefits are due to beta carotene itself, other nutrients in beta carotene–rich foods, other dietary habits, or other, nondietary lifestyle characteristics. Long-term, large, randomized trials can provide a direct test of the efficacy of beta carotene in the prevention of cancer or cardiovascular disease.

principles.statement: Beta carotene supplementation is not effective in preventing cancer or cardiovascular disease in healthy, well-nourished men.
principles.rationale: A 12-year randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial showed virtually no differences in the incidence of malignant neoplasms, cardiovascular disease, or overall mortality between the supplemented group and the placebo group.
principles.source_quote: In this trial among healthy men, 12 years of supplementation with beta carotene produced neither benefit nor harm in terms of the incidence of malignant neoplasms, cardiovascular disease, or death from all causes.

principles.statement: Public health recommendations must be based on definitive positive results or informative null findings from rigorous trials.
principles.rationale: Because the general public spends a substantial amount of money on supplements, it is critical to provide reliable, trial-backed evidence rather than relying on plausible mechanisms or observational correlations.
principles.source_quote: Our chief aim in this trial was to provide either a definitive positive result or an informative null finding on which to base rational public health recommendations.

signature_quotes.text: In this trial among healthy men, 12 years of supplementation with beta carotene produced neither benefit nor harm in terms of the incidence of malignant neoplasms, cardiovascular disease, or death from all causes.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding the findings of the 12-year Physicians' Health Study on beta carotene.

signature_quotes.text: Our chief aim in this trial was to provide either a definitive positive result or an informative null finding on which to base rational public health recommendations.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the core motivation behind conducting large-scale, long-term randomized trials.

signature_quotes.text: It is difficult to determine from observational studies, however, whether the apparent benefits are due to beta carotene itself, other nutrients in beta carotene–rich foods, other dietary habits, or other, nondietary lifestyle characteristics.
signature_quotes.context: Highlighting the limitations of observational epidemiology when studying dietary interventions.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on observational studies to make public health recommendations about dietary supplements.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Observational studies suffer from confounding variables; it is impossible to know if the observed health benefit comes from the specific nutrient being studied, other components of the diet, or broader healthy lifestyle factors common among people who eat nutrient-rich foods.
